questions.text: Who drafted the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 and limited the powers of the English King.
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was indeed signed in 1215 by King John.

questions.text: Which article of the Indian Constitution grants Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Articles 12–35 of the Indian Constitution deal with Fundamental Rights.

questions.text: Match the independence leaders with their independence movements:
questions.explanation: These leaders are associated with their respective freedom movements.

questions.text: The Sepoy Mutiny occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 began in May 1857.

questions.text: The French Revolution took place in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789.

questions.text: Which of these were Mughal emperors? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Akbar, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb were Mughal emperors.

questions.text: Assertion: Democracy requires a constitution. Reason: A constitution defines the structure and powers of government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

questions.text: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in which year?
questions.explanation: The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution currently has ______ Schedules.
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India currently contains 12 Schedules.

questions.text: In which year did the Indian Independence Act come into effect, granting independence to India?
questions.explanation: India gained independence on August 15, 1947, when the Indian Independence Act 1947 came into effect, ending colonial rule.

questions.text: Who was the first Prime Minister of independent India?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru became the first Prime Minister of independent India, serving from 1947 until his death in 1964.

questions.text: The first Battle of Panipat was fought in 1526.
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat was fought on 21 April 1526 between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi dynasty.

questions.text: In which year was the Indian Constitution adopted?
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution was adopted on November 26, 1949, and came into effect on January 26, 1950.

questions.text: The Salt March (Namak Satyagraha) was led by which leader?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi led the Salt March in 1930 from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi to protest British salt monopoly, making it a landmark civil disobedience movement.

questions.text: Who was the first President of independent India?
questions.explanation: Rajendra Prasad served as the first President of India from 1950 to 1962.

questions.text: Which of the following were key leaders of the Indian Independence Movement? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Gandhi, Bose, and Tilak were key independence leaders. Roy and Sen were social reformers of the 19th century, though they contributed to the reform movement.

questions.text: The Quit India Movement was launched in which year?
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement was launched on August 8, 1942, demanding immediate British withdrawal from India.

questions.text: Assertion: The Civil Disobedience Movement was initiated with the Salt March. Reason: Salt was a commodity monopolized by the British government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Gandhi chose the salt monopoly as the focus because it affected every Indian and was a just cause for non-violent protest.

questions.text: Who founded the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanation: Allan Octavian Hume, a British official, founded the Indian National Congress in 1885. Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee was its first president.

questions.text: The Act of Union merged Scotland with ________ in 1707.
questions.explanation: The Act of Union 1707 unified the kingdoms of Scotland and England to form the Kingdom of Great Britain.

questions.text: Who was known as the 'Father of the Nation' in India?
questions.explanation: Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (Mahatma Gandhi) is revered as the Father of the Nation for his role in India's independence struggle.

questions.text: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, when British General Dyer ordered troops to fire on unarmed Indian civilians.

questions.text: The United Nations was established immediately after World War I.
questions.explanation: The United Nations was established in 1945 after World War II, replacing the League of Nations.

questions.text: Which ancient civilization built the Colosseum?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum, an iconic amphitheater, was built by the Roman Empire in Rome between 72-80 AD.

questions.text: Who was the founder of the Mauryan Empire in India?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan Empire around 322 BCE. His grandson Ashoka later became the most famous Mauryan emperor.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in which year?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was signed on June 15, 1215, limiting the powers of King John and establishing the principle that everyone, including the king, is subject to law.

questions.text: Match the following Indian dynasties with their famous rulers:
questions.explanation: Ashoka was a Mauryan emperor, Samudragupta belonged to the Gupta dynasty, Akbar was a Mughal emperor, and Shivaji founded the Maratha Empire.

questions.text: Which emperor of the Mughal Empire is known for building the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan commissioned the construction of the Taj Mahal in Agra, dedicating it to his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.text: The Battle of Plassey (1757) was fought between the British and whom?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey in 1757 was fought between the British East India Company and the Nawab of Bengal, Siraj ud-Daulah.

questions.text: Assertion: Ashoka spread Buddhism across Asia. Reason: He was deeply affected by the bloodshed of the Kalinga War.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true. After witnessing the devastation of the Kalinga War, Ashoka embraced Buddhism and became a peace-promoting leader.

questions.text: Who was the founder of the Sikh religion?
questions.explanation: Guru Nanak Dev (1469-1539) was the founder of Sikhism, the fifth largest religion in the world.

questions.text: The Renaissance first began in Italy during the 14th century.
questions.explanation: The Italian Renaissance began in the 14th century, marking the transition from medieval to modern Europe.

questions.text: Which document was signed to end World War I?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles (1919) ended World War I and imposed harsh terms on Germany.

questions.text: The American Civil War was fought between which two regions?
options: D. B and C are both correct
questions.explanation: The American Civil War (1861-1865) was fought between the North (Union) and South (Confederate States) over slavery and states' rights.

questions.text: The printing press was invented by ________ in the 15th century.
questions.explanation: Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable type printing press around 1440, revolutionizing the spread of information.

questions.text: Which of the following were impacts of the Industrial Revolution? (Select all correct)
options: A. Shift from agrarian to industrial economy
B. Rise of factories and mechanization
E. Urbanization and migration to cities
questions.explanation: A, B, C, E are all impacts of the Industrial Revolution. D is false; global trade actually increased during this period.

questions.text: Who was the leader of the Nazi Party and dictator of Germany during World War II?
questions.explanation: Adolf Hitler led the Nazi Party and ruled Germany from 1933 until his death in 1945, orchestrating World War II and the Holocaust.

questions.text: In which year did India become a Republic?
questions.explanation: India became a sovereign democratic republic on January 26, 1950, when the Constitution came into effect.

questions.text: Assertion: The Khilafat Movement was led by Ali Brothers. Reason: It aimed to protect the Ottoman Caliph's authority.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true. Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali led the Khilafat Movement (1919-1924) to protect the Ottoman Caliph after WWI.

questions.text: Who wrote the Declaration of Independence of the United States?
questions.explanation: Thomas Jefferson drafted the Declaration of Independence in 1776, which declared American independence from British rule.

questions.text: The Great Wall of China was built primarily to defend against invasions from the north.
questions.explanation: The Great Wall of China was constructed over centuries to protect Chinese states from invasions and raids from nomadic groups in the north.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder of the world is located in Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only surviving ancient wonder of the world, located near Cairo, Egypt.

questions.text: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched in which year?
questions.explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920 to protest British policies in India.

questions.text: The Roman Empire was divided into Eastern and Western parts during the reign of ________.
questions.explanation: Emperor Diocletian (284-305 AD) divided the Roman Empire into Eastern and Western halves for better governance.

questions.text: Who was the founder of the Ottoman Empire?
questions.explanation: Osman I founded the Ottoman Empire in the early 14th century, which lasted until 1922.

questions.text: Match the following historical events with their years:
questions.explanation: These are landmark events in world history with their corresponding years.

questions.text: Which Indian freedom fighter was known as the 'Grand Old Man of India'?
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji, an anti-imperialist and one of the founders of the Indian National Congress, was called the 'Grand Old Man of India'.

questions.text: Nelson Mandela served as the first black president of South Africa.
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela became South Africa's first black head of state, serving as president from 1994 to 1999.

questions.text: Who was the first Governor-General of independent India?
questions.explanation: Earl Mountbatten served as the first Governor-General of independent India from 1947 to 1948.

questions.text: Assertion: The British abolished the practice of Sati in India. Reason: Raja Ram Mohan Roy campaigned against it.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true. Though Roy campaigned against it, the British legally abolished Sati in 1829 under Governor-General Bentinck.

questions.text: The Partition of India occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: India was partitioned on August 15, 1947, resulting in the creation of India and Pakistan as separate nations.

questions.text: The Indus Valley Civilization flourished during the ________ Age.
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization (2300-1300 BCE) was a Bronze Age civilization located in the Indian subcontinent.

questions.text: Which Chinese dynasty built the Great Wall?
questions.explanation: The Great Wall was built and rebuilt by multiple Chinese dynasties over centuries, with the most famous sections built during the Ming Dynasty.

questions.text: Which of the following were social reformers in 19th century India? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: A, B, C, E were social reformers. Lala Lajpat Rai was both a social reformer and nationalist, but primarily known as a freedom fighter.

questions.text: Who was the founder of the Vijayanagara Empire?
questions.explanation: Harihara I founded the Vijayanagara Empire in 1336, though his brother Bukka Raya I helped consolidate it.

questions.text: The Chinese Revolution led by Mao Zedong occurred in 1949.
questions.explanation: The Chinese Communist Revolution, led by Mao Zedong, established the People's Republic of China on October 1, 1949.

questions.text: Which event is generally considered to have sparked the beginning of World War I?
options: B. The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
C. The sinking of the Lusitania
D. The bombing of Pearl Harbor
questions.explanation: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in June 1914 was the immediate catalyst that started World War I.

questions.text: Match the following ancient civilizations with their major rivers:
questions.explanation: These civilizations developed along major river systems that provided water, fertile soil, and transportation.

questions.text: Who revived and led the Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943 during World War II?
questions.explanation: While Mohan Singh initially formed the INA in 1942, Subhas Chandra Bose revived and reorganized it in 1943 to fight for India's independence.
